title: Rajaraja's Sword: Kandalur to Lanka
subtitle: From the burning salai of Kandalur to the fallen throne of Anuradhapura
region: South India, Kerala and Sri Lanka
moral: Strength is worthy only when it builds order, not just victory.

sources.citation: K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas, ch. on Rajaraja I
Rajaraja's meykkirti inscriptions (Kandalur Salai, Ilam/Lanka, the Pandya war)

panels.shot: epic low-angle wide of a warship prowed with a tiger emblem cutting through dark waves toward a distant island city

dialogue.text: Rajaraja Chola I, lord of the Kaveri, bears his sword from Kandalur’s burning salai to the ancient throne of Lanka in the age of rising empires.

panels.shot: wide interior of a dim pillared hall in Thanjavur, lit by oil lamps, with maps spread before a king

dialogue.text: In the late tenth century, the Kaveri valley glowed with lamps, temples, and trade. Yet Rajaraja, newly crowned in Thanjavur, saw beyond its river fields to seas and rival crowns.

panels.shot: medium close-up of Rajaraja in council, profile view, with distant sea imagined behind him

dialogue.text: To the west lay the Chera’s coast and their famed salai of Kandalur, a fortified seat of warriors and ships. To the south, the old Pandya crown smouldered, backed by friends across the sea in Lanka.

panels.shot: sweeping bird’s-eye view of the Kaveri delta shipyards with the king inspecting

dialogue.text: Rajaraja did not rush to war. He counted land, grain, and men, reformed his levies, and turned river craftsmen to the building of ships. The Chola tiger would learn to swim.

panels.shot: low-angle wide of Kandalur coastline with Chola fleet offshore at dawn

dialogue.text: His meykkirti would later boast: he destroyed Kandalur Salai, where the Chera once fed their warriors. Before the temple-fed warriors could march, Chola ships and swords crept upon their coast.

panels.shot: dynamic mid-shot of Rajaraja leading troops through smoke toward the salai

dialogue.text: Kandalur’s salai fell. Its halls, once rich with gifts of kings, now cracked with flame. With that blow, Rajaraja drove a wedge into Chera pride and claimed the western sea for his fleets.

panels.shot: medium shot in a quiet camp at night with Rajaraja seated by a lamp, map of the south laid out

dialogue.text: From Kandalur, his gaze turned inward again. The ancient Pandya land, weakened but proud, leaned on Lankan kings and their gold. The Chola saw that to secure his river, he must steady the south.

panels.shot: epic wide shot of Chola army advancing toward a distant city representing Madurai

dialogue.text: Rajaraja marched on the Pandyas. His inscriptions remember how he took their crowns and slew their power, binding their land as feudatories. Each captured banner was another knot in his growing net of order.

panels.shot: medium shot inside a captured Pandya hall, with Rajaraja receiving submission

dialogue.text: Yet he did not merely break. He bound. Grants were written, temples endowed, officers installed. The Chola system reached into village and shrine, tightening the weave of authority over the southern plains.

panels.shot: wide shot of a coastal port with Chola fleet being loaded, Lanka visible as a faint silhouette

dialogue.text: Beyond the southern cape lay Lanka, where the Anuradhapura kings had long tangled in Pandya affairs. In time, Rajaraja turned his new navy and hardened legions toward that island of ancient monasteries and wealth.

panels.shot: dramatic low-angle of Chola ships mid-sea in rough waters

dialogue.text: It was a bold thing, to carry war over the sea. But the strait between Kaveri and Lanka narrowed under his will. His meykkirti would later proclaim: he took Ilam, seizing the long-renowned Anuradhapura.

panels.shot: wide shot of Chola troops landing on Lanka’s shore and advancing inland

dialogue.text: The Lankan chronicles, in their own tongue, speak of foreign hosts and burning shrines. Between Chola record and island memory, one truth stands clear: a great capital was taken, and the island’s order was broken.

panels.shot: epic wide of Anuradhapura’s ruined city with Chola banners rising

dialogue.text: Anuradhapura, seat of centuries of Lankan kings, fell to his hand. Its royal line fled, its treasures and sacred relics were seized or scattered. The island’s balance tilted toward the Kaveri king.

panels.shot: medium interior of a commandeered Lankan hall with Rajaraja issuing orders to administrators

dialogue.text: Victory brought another task: rule. Garrisons were placed, taxes assessed, shrines both controlled and endowed. Rajaraja’s rule in Lanka was firm but not whole, held through force, revenue, and the sword’s shadow.

panels.shot: medium shot of Rajaraja back in Thanjavur, overseeing temple foundations (Brihadisvara in early stages)

dialogue.text: His sword crossed seas, but its gains flowed into stone and order at home. Land from Pandya and Lanka fed endowments. In Thanjavur, the great temple rose, its lamps fuelled by conquered fields and ports.

panels.shot: close-up on an inscription being carved, Rajaraja’s figure faint in the background

dialogue.text: In his meykkirtis, he had the wars named: Kandalur Salai burned, Pandyas subdued, Ilam taken. But the same walls spoke of gifts, weights, fields, and regulations. Power was set down as record, not only as boast.

panels.shot: medium shot of Rajaraja in a quieter old age, overlooking the Kaveri from a balcony at dusk

dialogue.speaker: rajaraja_chola_i
dialogue.text: Let the seas know our strength, and our villages know our justice.

panels.shot: epic high-angle montage of the Chola world: Kaveri temples, Kerala coast, and Lanka, linked by sea routes

dialogue.text: Rajaraja’s age tied river, coast, and island into one imperial web. His sword humbled Kandalur, Pandya, and Lanka—but his lasting mark lay in the order he laid across the waters and lands of the south.
